title: Listening - Part 3
description: You will hear an interview with John and Meredith about sustainable architecture. For questions 15–20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best.
instructions: For questions 15–20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best.

extracts.context: An interview with John and Meredith discussing sustainable architecture.
extracts.audio_script:
Full interview 400–500 words. Mark answer evidence: [15]...[/15] through [20]...[/20]. Use 

 for paragraph breaks within a long turn.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Today, we're discussing sustainable architecture with John and Meredith, both experts in the field. So, John, what initially inspired you to focus on sustainable design?

dialogue.speaker: john
dialogue.text: Well, I noticed a significant shift towards eco-friendly practices in construction about a decade ago. The idea of creating buildings that harmoniously interact with their environment was particularly compelling to me. It was a movement that seemed not only necessary but inevitable.

dialogue.speaker: meredith
dialogue.text: For me, it was more about ensuring that the spaces we create don't just exist in harmony with their environment but actively contribute back. I was deeply influenced by community projects that I saw having a real, lasting impact on both people and nature.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Meredith, could you elaborate on a project that exemplifies your approach?

dialogue.speaker: meredith
dialogue.text: Absolutely. One of my proudest projects was a community center that incorporated solar energy, rainwater harvesting, and green roofs, all designed to reduce the building's footprint while serving the community's needs. It was a collaboration that really showcased how innovative design can benefit everyone involved.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: John, what's the biggest challenge you face in sustainable architecture?

dialogue.speaker: john
dialogue.text: I think one major challenge is changing perceptions. Many people still see sustainable design as an expensive alternative, when in fact, the long-term savings and benefits can be substantial. It's about education and demonstrating those benefits clearly.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What developments do you both see on the horizon for sustainable architecture?

dialogue.speaker: meredith
dialogue.text: I'm excited about the advances in materials technology. We're seeing innovations that make sustainable options much more viable and cost-effective, which in turn, makes them more appealing to a wider audience.

dialogue.speaker: john
dialogue.text: Yes, I'm optimistic about the integration of smart technology in sustainable designs. I believe it's going to play a critical role in optimizing energy use and efficiency across all types of buildings, effectively marrying the ecological benefits with technological advancements.

questions.question: What initially inspired John to focus on sustainable design?
options: C) The trend towards eco-friendly practices
questions.explanation: John was inspired by the shift towards eco-friendly practices.

questions.question: Meredith's projects aim to:
options: A) Harmoniously exist with the environment
questions.explanation: Meredith focuses on projects that harmoniously exist with and benefit the environment.

questions.question: The community center project Meredith describes focuses on:
questions.explanation: Meredith's project reduces environmental impact through various eco-friendly features.

questions.question: What does John identify as a major challenge in sustainable architecture?
questions.explanation: John sees changing public perceptions as a key challenge.

questions.question: Meredith is optimistic about:
questions.explanation: Meredith mentions advances in materials technology as promising.

questions.question: How does John view the role of technology in sustainable architecture's future?
questions.explanation: John believes smart technology will be crucial for energy efficiency.
